API Reference

Document & Slide

Document — a complete report container holding multiple slides.

class reporting.document.Document(title: str, author: str = '', theme: Theme = <factory>, slides: list[Slide] = <factory>)[source]

Bases: object

A report document that holds slides and coordinates rendering.

Document is the top-level container. Add slides to it and then call render() with a renderer backend.

Parameters:

  • title – Report title (displayed in metadata, not on slides).

  • author – Author name for metadata (default "").

  • theme – Visual theme applied to all slides by default. Built-in choices: CorporateTheme(), DarkTheme(), LightTheme() (default CorporateTheme()).

  • slides – Initial list of slides. Prefer add_slide() or new_slide() instead of setting this directly.

Example:

from reporting.document import Document
from reporting.slide import Slide
from reporting.renderers.pdf.renderer import PDFRenderer

doc = Document("My Report", author="Engineer")
slide = Slide("Introduction")
slide.grid_layout(rows=1, cols=1)
slide[0, 0].text("Hello, world!")
doc.add_slide(slide)
doc.render(PDFRenderer(), "output.pdf")
add_slide(slide: Slide) → Slide[source]

Append an already-built Slide to the document.

Parameters:

slide – A fully configured Slide instance (grid layout and content already set).

Returns:

The same slide instance (useful for chaining).

Example:

doc = Document("Report")
s = Slide("Page 1")
s.grid_layout(rows=1, cols=1)
s[0, 0].text("Content")
doc.add_slide(s)
new_slide(title: str, subtitle: str | None = None, theme: Theme | None = None, width: float = 960.0, height: float = 540.0, slide_type: str = 'default', base_slide: Slide | None = None) → Slide[source]

Create a Slide, add it to the document, and return it.

Shorthand for Slide(...) + add_slide(...).

Parameters:

  • title – Slide title (shown in the title panel).

  • subtitle – Optional subtitle shown below the title (default None).

  • theme – Visual theme for this slide. Falls back to the document-level theme if None (default None).

  • width – Slide width in pixels (default 960.0 — standard 4:3).

  • height – Slide height in pixels (default 540.0 — standard 4:3).

  • slide_type – Name of a pre-defined slide type in the theme (default "default").

  • base_slide – Another Slide whose config and grid layout are used as a starting point (default None).

Returns:

The newly created Slide, already appended to the document.

Example:

doc = Document("Report")
slide = doc.new_slide("Results", subtitle="Test data")
slide.grid_layout(rows=2, cols=2)
slide[0, 0].text("Cell A")
render(renderer: BaseRenderer, output_path: str) → None[source]

Render the document to a file using the given backend.

Parameters:

  • renderer – A renderer instance such as PDFRenderer() or HTMLRenderer().

  • output_path – Destination file path (e.g. "report.pdf" or "report.html"). The renderer creates or overwrites this file.

Example:

from reportlab.renderers.pdf.renderer import PDFRenderer
doc.render(PDFRenderer(), "final_report.pdf")

Slide — a single page in a report with grid layout and NumPy-style cell access.

class reporting.slide.Slide(title: str, subtitle: str | None = None, theme: Theme | None = None, width: float = 960.0, height: float = 540.0, title_panel_height: float | None = None, background: str | SolidBackground | GradientBackground | ImageBackground | None = None, title_config: TitleConfig | None = None, subtitle_config: SubtitleConfig | None = None, title_panel_config: TitlePanelConfig | None = None, footer_config: FooterConfig | None = None, footer_logo: str | None = None, slide_type: str = 'default', base_slide: Slide | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A single slide (page) in a report.

Every Slide has a built-in title panel rendered automatically at the top. The remaining area below it is divided into a grid via grid_layout().

Cell access uses NumPy-style indexing:

slide[row, col]          # single cell
slide[0, :]              # entire first row
slide[:, 0]              # entire first column
slide[0:2, 1:3]          # sub-grid
Parameters:

  • title – Slide title displayed in the title panel.

  • subtitle – Optional subtitle (default None).

  • theme – Visual theme. Falls back to CorporateTheme() when None (default None).

  • width – Slide width in pixels (default 960.0).

  • height – Slide height in pixels (default 540.0).

  • title_panel_height – Height of the title panel in pixels. Falls back to the slide type default when None (default None).

  • background – Slide background. Accepts a hex string "#RRGGBB", a named CSS color, or a SolidBackground / GradientBackground / ImageBackground instance (default None).

  • title_config – Configure title font, size, colour, and separator. Falls back to the slide type default when None (default None).

  • subtitle_config – Configure subtitle font and colour. Falls back to the slide type default when None (default None).

  • title_panel_config – Configure subtitle placement and padding. Falls back to the slide type default when None (default None).

  • footer_config – Footer styling and content. Falls back to the slide type default when None (default None).

  • footer_logo – Optional path to a logo image in the left footer cell (default None).

  • slide_type – Name of a pre-defined slide type in the theme (default "default"). Ignored when a base_slide is given without an explicit override.

  • base_slide – Another Slide whose config and grid layout are used as a starting point. Content cells are not copied (default None).

Example:

from reporting.slide import Slide

slide = Slide("Results", subtitle="Test data")
slide.grid_layout(rows=2, cols=2, gap=8)
slide[0, 0].text("Top-left", bold=True)
slide[1, :].text("Spanning bottom row")

Text can also inherit all font properties from the slide’s theme:

slide[0, 0].text("Heading", style="h1")
slide[0, 1].text("Body text", style="body",
                  color=palette.primary.css)

Use a pre-defined slide type from the theme:

slide = Slide("Cover", slide_type="title")

Or base a slide on another slide to reuse its layout:

base = Slide("Master")
base.grid_layout(rows=2, cols=3, gap=10)

child = Slide("Child", base_slide=base)
# child has the same theme, header/footer config,
# background, and 2x3 grid layout
property content_size: Size

Return the available area below the title panel.

When a footer grid has been set up via footer_layout(), the footer height is also subtracted.

Returns:

Size(width, height - title_panel_height[- footer_height]) in pixels.

property footer: _FooterProxy

Access the footer grid.

Returns:

A _FooterProxy for NumPy-style indexing into footer cells.

Raises:

RuntimeError – If footer_layout() has not been called first.

footer_layout(rows: int = 1, cols: int = 4, row_sizes: list[LengthValue | float] | None = None, col_sizes: list[LengthValue | float] | None = None, gap: float = 0.0, padding: Edges | None = None) → None[source]

Divide the footer area into a grid of cells.

Must be called before accessing footer cells with slide.footer[r, c].

Parameters:

  • rows – Number of rows (default 1).

  • cols – Number of columns (default 4).

  • row_sizes – Per-row sizing (default None = all rows use Fill).

  • col_sizes – Per-column sizing (default None = all columns use Fill).

  • gap – Spacing between cells in pixels (default 0.0).

  • padding – Outer padding around the footer grid (default None = no extra padding; the footer’s own FooterConfig.padding is applied by the renderer).

Example:

slide.footer_layout(rows=1, cols=4, gap=4)
slide.footer[0, 0].page_number(style="caption")
slide.footer[0, 1].text(" | ", style="caption")
slide.footer[0, 2].total_pages(style="caption")
get_cell_rects() → list[list[Rect]][source]

Compute the pixel rectangles of every grid cell.

Raises:

RuntimeError – If grid_layout() has not been called.

Returns:

A 2-D list of Rect, one per cell in the grid.

get_footer_cell_rects() → list[list[Rect]][source]

Compute the pixel rectangles of every footer grid cell.

Rectangles are relative to the top-left of the footer content area (inside FooterConfig.padding).

Returns:

A 2-D list of Rect, one per cell in the footer grid.

grid_layout(rows: int = 1, cols: int = 1, row_sizes: list[LengthValue | float] | None = None, col_sizes: list[LengthValue | float] | None = None, gap: float = 0.0, padding: Edges | None = None) → None[source]

Divide the content area into a grid of cells.

Must be called before accessing cells with slide[r, c].

Parameters:

  • rows – Number of rows (default 1).

  • cols – Number of columns (default 1).

  • row_sizes – Per-row sizing. Each element can be Px(v) for fixed, Percent(v) for percentage, Fill for remaining space, or a plain float treated as Px(v) (default None = all rows use Fill).

  • col_sizes – Per-column sizing, same rules as row_sizes (default None = all columns use Fill).

  • gap – Spacing between cells in pixels (default 0.0).

  • padding – Outer padding around the grid as an Edges. Edges.all(v) sets uniform padding (default None = no padding).

Example:

slide.grid_layout(rows=3, cols=2, gap=8,
                  padding=Edges.all(12))
slide[0, 0].text("Cell (0, 0)")
property size: Size

Return the full slide dimensions as a Size.

Returns:

Size(width, height) in pixels.

Layout

Pure geometry primitives — no visual representation concepts, no renderer imports.

class reporting.layout.geometry.Edges(left: float = 0.0, top: float = 0.0, right: float = 0.0, bottom: float = 0.0)[source]

Bases: object

Four-sided offset (padding, margin, or border widths).

Each side can be set independently. Factory methods all() and symmetric() provide common shortcuts.

Parameters:

  • left – Left edge offset (default 0.0).

  • top – Top edge offset (default 0.0).

  • right – Right edge offset (default 0.0).

  • bottom – Bottom edge offset (default 0.0).

Example:

from reporting.layout.geometry import Edges

uniform = Edges.all(8)
symmetric = Edges.symmetric(horizontal=12, vertical=6)
custom = Edges(left=20, top=10, right=20, bottom=10)
static all(value: float) → Edges[source]

Create an instance with the same value on all four sides.

Parameters:

value – Offset applied to left, top, right, and bottom.

Returns:

Edges(value, value, value, value).

property horizontal: float

Sum of left and right offsets.

static symmetric(horizontal: float = 0.0, vertical: float = 0.0) → Edges[source]

Create an instance with mirrored horizontal/vertical values.

Parameters:

  • horizontal – Value for left and right (default 0.0).

  • vertical – Value for top and bottom (default 0.0).

Returns:

Edges(horizontal, vertical, horizontal, vertical).

property vertical: float

Sum of top and bottom offsets.

class reporting.layout.geometry.Point(x: float, y: float)[source]

Bases: NamedTuple

A 2-D point in layout coordinates.

Parameters:

  • x – X-coordinate (horizontal, left-to-right).

  • y – Y-coordinate (vertical, top-to-bottom).

Example:

from reporting.layout.geometry import Point

p = Point(100.0, 200.0)
x: float

Alias for field number 0

y: float

Alias for field number 1

class reporting.layout.geometry.Rect(x: float, y: float, width: float, height: float)[source]

Bases: object

An axis-aligned rectangle defined by origin and extent.

The coordinate system is top-left origin (y increases downward).

Parameters:

  • x – Left edge X-coordinate.

  • y – Top edge Y-coordinate.

  • width – Horizontal extent.

  • height – Vertical extent.

Example:

from reporting.layout.geometry import Rect, Point

r = Rect(10, 20, 100, 50)
r.inset(5, 5, 5, 5)          # shrink uniformly
r.contains_point(Point(30, 40))  # True
property bottom: float

Bottom edge Y-coordinate (y + height).

property bottom_right: Point

Return the bottom-right corner as a Point.

contains_point(p: Point) → bool[source]

Test whether a point lies within (or on the boundary of) the rectangle.

Parameters:

p – The point to test.

Returns:

True if p is inside the rectangle.

inset(left: float, top: float, right: float, bottom: float) → Rect[source]

Shrink the rectangle by offsets on each side.

Parameters:

  • left – Amount to subtract from x and width.

  • top – Amount to subtract from y and height.

  • right – Amount to subtract from width (right side).

  • bottom – Amount to subtract from height (bottom side).

Returns:

A new Rect with the inset applied.

property left: float

Left edge X-coordinate (same as x).

property right: float

Right edge X-coordinate (x + width).

property size: Size

Return the rectangle extent as a Size.

property top: float

Top edge Y-coordinate (same as y).

property top_left: Point

Return the top-left corner as a Point.

class reporting.layout.geometry.Size(width: float, height: float)[source]

Bases: NamedTuple

A 2-D extent (width and height).

Parameters:

  • width – Horizontal dimension in points / pixels.

  • height – Vertical dimension in points / pixels.

Example:

from reporting.layout.geometry import Size

sz = Size(960.0, 540.0)
height: float

Alias for field number 1

width: float

Alias for field number 0

Sizing primitives for layout — pure math, no renderer imports.

reporting.layout.sizing.Auto = LengthValue(value=0.0, sizing_type=<SizingType.AUTO: 'auto'>)

LengthValue sized by content (not resolved at layout time).

reporting.layout.sizing.Fill = LengthValue(value=1.0, sizing_type=<SizingType.FILL: 'fill'>)

LengthValue that fills remaining space (flex-grow weight = 1).

class reporting.layout.sizing.LengthValue(value: float, sizing_type: SizingType)[source]

Bases: object

A length specification with a value and sizing strategy.

Create instances via the factory functions Px(), Percent(), or by using the module-level constants Auto / Fill.

Parameters:

  • value – Numeric value (units depend on sizing_type).

  • sizing_type – The strategy that interprets value.

Example:

from reporting.layout.sizing import LengthValue, Px, Percent, Fill

fixed = Px(120)            # exactly 120 pt
pct = Percent(50)          # 50 % of available space
grow = Fill                # flex-grow with weight 1
resolve(available: float, total_fill_weight: float, own_fill_weight: float) → float[source]

Compute the resolved length for a given available space.

Parameters:

  • available – Total available space along this axis.

  • total_fill_weight – Sum of value across all FILL entries on this axis.

  • own_fill_weight – This entry’s value (used as a flex ratio).

Returns:

Resolved length in points / pixels.

reporting.layout.sizing.Percent(value: float) → LengthValue[source]

Create a percentage-based length.

Parameters:

value – Percentage (0–100) of the available space.

Returns:

LengthValue(value, SizingType.PERCENT).

reporting.layout.sizing.Px(value: float) → LengthValue[source]

Create a fixed-size length in points / pixels.

Parameters:

value – Exact length.

Returns:

LengthValue(value, SizingType.FIXED).

reporting.layout.sizing.Sizing

A length that can be expressed as a LengthValue or a raw float (treated as Px(value)).

alias of LengthValue | float

class reporting.layout.sizing.SizingType(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

The strategy used to resolve a length value.

Members:

AUTO

Dimension determined by content size (not yet resolved at layout time).

FILL

Dimension takes a fraction of the available space proportional to its weight (flex-grow).

FIXED

Dimension is an exact value in points / pixels.

PERCENT

Dimension is a percentage of the available space.

reporting.layout.sizing.normalize(value: LengthValue | float) → LengthValue[source]

Convert a Sizing to a LengthValue.

Floats and ints are wrapped in Px().

Parameters:

value – A LengthValue or a plain number.

Returns:

A LengthValue instance.

Layout constraints — pure math, no renderer imports.

class reporting.layout.constraints.AxisConstraints(min_size: float = 0.0, max_size: float = inf)[source]

Bases: object

Min / max constraints for a single axis.

Parameters:

  • min_size – Minimum allowed size (default 0.0).

  • max_size – Maximum allowed size (default float("inf")).

Example:

from reporting.layout.constraints import AxisConstraints

ax = AxisConstraints(min_size=20, max_size=200)
ax.clamp(10)    # → 20
ax.clamp(300)   # → 200
ax.clamp(100)   # → 100
clamp(value: float) → float[source]

Clamp a value to the allowed range.

Parameters:

value – The value to clamp.

Returns:

value if within [min_size, max_size], otherwise the nearest bound.

class reporting.layout.constraints.Constraints(width: AxisConstraints = <factory>, height: AxisConstraints = <factory>, min_aspect_ratio: float | None = None, max_aspect_ratio: float | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

2-D constraints with optional aspect-ratio bounds.

Parameters:

  • width – Width axis constraints (default AxisConstraints() = unbounded).

  • height – Height axis constraints (default AxisConstraints() = unbounded).

  • min_aspect_ratio – Minimum width / height ratio (default None = no minimum).

  • max_aspect_ratio – Maximum width / height ratio (default None = no maximum).

Example:

from reporting.layout.constraints import Constraints, AxisConstraints

c = Constraints(
    width=AxisConstraints(min_size=50, max_size=400),
    height=AxisConstraints(min_size=30, max_size=300),
    min_aspect_ratio=0.5,
    max_aspect_ratio=2.0,
)
c.clamp(500, 100)  # → (400, 200) — width clamped, aspect ratio enforced
clamp(w: float, h: float) → tuple[float, float][source]

Clamp width and height and enforce aspect-ratio bounds.

Aspect-ratio enforcement may adjust the width further after the independent axis clamps.

Parameters:

  • w – Proposed width.

  • h – Proposed height.

Returns:

(w, h) clamped and aspect-ratio adjusted.

Panel — a rectangular region within a grid layout.

No renderer dependencies. Pure layout geometry.

class reporting.layout.panel.HAlign(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

Horizontal alignment options for content within a cell.

Members:

LEFT: Align content to the left edge of the cell. CENTER: Centre content horizontally within the cell. RIGHT: Align content to the right edge of the cell. STRETCH: Scale/stretch content to fill the full cell

width (default).

class reporting.layout.panel.Panel(row: int = 0, col: int = 0, rowspan: int = 1, colspan: int = 1, padding: Edges = <factory>, margin: Edges = <factory>, background_color: ColorValue | None = None, border: str | None = None, border_radius: float = 0.0, h_align: HAlign = HAlign.STRETCH, v_align: VAlign = VAlign.STRETCH, constraints: Constraints = <factory>, min_size: Size = <factory>, fixed_size: Size | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A cell (or merged cell group) within the grid layout.

Panel stores layout metadata: position, span, padding, margin, background, border, alignment, and size constraints. It is created automatically when constructing a Grid.

Parameters:

  • row – Row index (default 0).

  • col – Column index (default 0).

  • rowspan – Number of rows this panel spans (default 1).

  • colspan – Number of columns this panel spans (default 1).

  • padding – Inner padding as an Edges instance (default Edges() = zero).

  • margin – Outer margin as an Edges instance (default Edges() = zero).

  • background_color – Background colour (any ColorValue) (default None = transparent).

  • border – CSS-style border shorthand or None (default None).

  • border_radius – Corner radius in points (default 0.0).

  • h_align – Horizontal alignment of child content (default HAlign.STRETCH).

  • v_align – Vertical alignment of child content (default VAlign.STRETCH).

  • constraints – Min/max size constraints (default Constraints() = unbounded).

  • min_size – Minimum size as Size(w, h) (default Size(0, 0)).

  • fixed_size – Fixed size override; when set, the panel ignores grid-computed sizing (default None).

Example:

from reporting.layout.panel import Panel, HAlign, VAlign
from reporting.layout.geometry import Edges

panel = Panel(
    row=0, col=0,
    padding=Edges.all(8),
    h_align=HAlign.CENTER,
    v_align=VAlign.MIDDLE,
)
property content_area: str

Return "panel" if a fixed_size is set, otherwise "dynamic".

class reporting.layout.panel.VAlign(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

Vertical alignment options for content within a cell.

Members:

TOP: Align content to the top edge of the cell. MIDDLE: Centre content vertically within the cell. BOTTOM: Align content to the bottom edge of the cell. STRETCH: Scale/stretch content to fill the full cell

height (default).

Grid layout engine — pure math, no renderer dependencies.

class reporting.layout.grid.Grid(rows: int, cols: int, row_sizes: list[LengthValue | float] | None = None, col_sizes: list[LengthValue | float] | None = None, gap: float = 0.0, padding: Edges | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

Rectangular grid that resolves row / column sizes and computes cell rectangles.

Created automatically by Slide.grid_layout(). Supports NumPy-style cell access via __getitem__, automatic span merging, and flexible sizing (fixed, percentage, fill).

Parameters:

  • rows – Number of rows.

  • cols – Number of columns.

  • row_sizes – Per-row sizing. Each element can be Px(v), Percent(v), Fill, or a plain float (treated as Px(v)). When None, all rows get Fill (equal height).

  • col_sizes – Per-column sizing (same rules as row_sizes). When None, all columns get Fill (equal width).

  • gap – Spacing between rows and columns in pixels (default 0.0).

  • padding – Outer padding around the grid (default Edges() = zero).

Example:

from reporting.layout.grid import Grid
from reporting.layout.geometry import Size
from reporting.layout.sizing import Px, Percent, Fill

grid = Grid(rows=3, cols=2,
            row_sizes=[Px(50), Fill, Percent(30)],
            col_sizes=[Percent(60), Fill],
            gap=8)
total, rects = grid.layout(Size(800, 400))
# rects[r][c] → Rect with resolved pixel positions
copy_structure() → Grid[source]

Create a new Grid with the same dimensions, sizing, gap, and padding, but with fresh empty cells.

Returns:

A new Grid sharing the structural properties of this one.

layout(available: Size) → tuple[Size, list[list[Rect]]][source]

Compute final cell positions for a given available area.

The algorithm:

  1. Subtracts padding and gaps from the available space.

  2. Resolves row heights and column widths from sizing specs (fixed → exact, percent → % of available, fill → proportional flex).

  3. Computes pixel rectangles for each cell.

  4. Merges rectangles for cells with rowspan > 1 or colspan > 1 (zeroes out covered cells).

Parameters:

available – The total Size available for the grid.

Returns:

A tuple (total_size, cell_rects) where cell_rects is a 2-D list[list[Rect]] (same shape as the grid). Merged cells have zero Rect(0, 0, 0, 0) at covered positions.

class reporting.layout.grid.GridCell(panel: Panel, element: object | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A single cell in a Grid, combining a Panel and an optional element.

Parameters:

  • panel – The cell’s Panel (layout metadata).

  • element – Content element (TextElement, ImageElement, etc.), set when a cell is populated (default None).

class reporting.layout.grid.GridPosition(row: int, col: int)[source]

Bases: object

Row / column coordinate within a Grid.

Not typically instantiated directly; used internally for cell look-up.

Parameters:

  • row – Row index.

  • col – Column index.

Elements

Base element type — backend-agnostic content representation.

class reporting.elements.base.BaseElement(element_type: ElementType, properties: dict[str, ~typing.Any]=<factory>)[source]

Bases: object

Abstract base for all content elements in a report.

Subclasses must set element_type to the corresponding ElementType member.

The properties dictionary stores arbitrary metadata (id, css_class, and any keyword arguments passed to the constructor).

Parameters:

  • element_type – The type tag for this element.

  • properties – User-supplied keyword arguments stored as a dict (default empty dict).

Example:

from reporting.elements.base import BaseElement, ElementType

el = BaseElement(ElementType.TEXT, {"bold": True})
class reporting.elements.base.ElementType(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

Discriminated union tag for all element subclasses.

Members:

TEXT: TextElement IMAGE: ImageElement FIGURE: FigureElement TABLE: TableElement TABLESPEC: TableSpecElement CONTAINER: ContainerElement SPACER: SpacerElement

Text element — rich text content, backend-agnostic.

class reporting.elements.text.TextAlignment(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

Horizontal alignment for a text block.

Members:

LEFT: Align text to the left edge. CENTER: Centre text horizontally. RIGHT: Align text to the right edge. JUSTIFY: Stretch text to fill the full width.

class reporting.elements.text.TextBlock(runs: list[TextRun], alignment: TextAlignment = TextAlignment.LEFT, spacing_before: float = 0.0, spacing_after: float = 0.0)[source]

Bases: object

A paragraph consisting of one or more TextRun instances.

Parameters:

  • runs – The runs that make up this paragraph.

  • alignment – Horizontal alignment (default TextAlignment.LEFT).

  • spacing_before – Extra space before the paragraph in points (default 0.0).

  • spacing_after – Extra space after the paragraph in points (default 0.0).

class reporting.elements.text.TextElement(text: str = '', **kwargs: object)[source]

Bases: BaseElement

A text content element consisting of one or more TextBlock instances.

When created via _CellProxy.text(), a single block with one run is constructed automatically from convenience kwargs (bold, italic, color, size, font_name, alignment). For mixed-format text, build runs and blocks manually after construction.

Parameters:

  • text – Initial plain text content (default "").

  • **kwargs –

    Formatting kwargs extracted at construction:

    • bold: bool

    • italic: bool

    • color: ColorValue

    • size: float (points)

    • font_name: str

    • alignment: TextAlignment or str

Example:

from reporting.elements.text import TextElement, TextRun, TextAlignment

# Simple — one style for the whole string
el = TextElement("Hello", bold=True, size=14)

# Mixed-format
el = TextElement()
el.add_run("Hello ", bold=True)
el.add_run("world", italic=True)
el.add_block(alignment=TextAlignment.CENTER)
el.add_run("Centered line")
add_block(alignment: TextAlignment = TextAlignment.LEFT, spacing_before: float = 0.0, spacing_after: float = 0.0) → TextBlock[source]

Start a new paragraph block.

Parameters:

  • alignment – Horizontal alignment (default TextAlignment.LEFT).

  • spacing_before – Space before in points (default 0.0).

  • spacing_after – Space after in points (default 0.0).

Returns:

The created (empty) TextBlock.

add_run(text: str, bold: bool = False, italic: bool = False, color: str | None = None, size: float | None = None, font_name: str | None = None) → TextRun[source]

Append a TextRun to the last block.

If no blocks exist yet, creates a TextBlock first.

Parameters:

  • text – Run text.

  • bold – Bold flag (default False).

  • italic – Italic flag (default False).

  • color – Colour (default None).

  • size – Font size in points (default None).

  • font_name – Font family (default None).

Returns:

The created TextRun.

class reporting.elements.text.TextRun(text: str, bold: bool = False, italic: bool = False, underline: bool = False, color: ColorValue | None = None, size: float | None = None, font_name: str | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A single run of text with uniform formatting.

Multiple runs within a TextBlock create mixed-format content (e.g. “Hello bold world”).

Parameters:

  • text – The text content.

  • bold – Whether the run is bold (default False).

  • italic – Whether the run is italic (default False).

  • underline – Whether the run is underlined (default False).

  • color – Text colour as a ColorValue (default None = inherit).

  • size – Font size in points (default None = inherit).

  • font_name – Font family (default None = inherit).

Example:

from reporting.elements.text import TextRun

run = TextRun("Hello", bold=True, size=14)

Image element — backend-agnostic image reference.

class reporting.elements.image.ImageElement(source: str = '', **kwargs: object)[source]

Bases: BaseElement

An image content element a raster or vector image file.

The image is loaded from a file path. Supported formats: PNG, JPG, and SVG (SVG requires HTML renderer).

Sizing priority (highest first): 1. Explicit width / height (points) 2. fit_mode (FIT_VERTICAL / FIT_HORIZONTAL) 3. scale multiplier applied to the native size 4. ORIGINAL — native size, scaled down only if

larger than the container

Parameters:

  • source – Path to the image file. Extension is used to auto-detect image_format.

  • **kwargs –

    Property overrides:

    • alt_text: str accessiblity text (default "")

    • scale: float uniform scale factor (default 1.0)

    • rotation: float clockwise rotation in degrees (default 0.0)

    • opacity: float from 0.0 (transparent) to 1.0 (opaque) (default 1.0)

    • width: float explicit width in points (default None)

    • height: float explicit height in points (default None)

    • fit_mode: ImageFitMode or str (default "original")

Example:

from reporting.elements.image import ImageElement, ImageFitMode

el = ImageElement("chart.png", scale=0.8)
el2 = ImageElement("photo.jpg",
                  fit_mode=ImageFitMode.FIT_VERTICAL)
el3 = ImageElement("logo.png", width=120, height=60)
class reporting.elements.image.ImageFitMode(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

How an image is fitted into its container cell.

Members:
ORIGINAL: Keep native size; scale down (maintaining

aspect ratio) only if larger than the container in any dimension.

FIT_VERTICAL: Scale to fill the container’s height;

width follows aspect ratio.

FIT_HORIZONTAL: Scale to fill the container’s width;

height follows aspect ratio.

class reporting.elements.image.ImageFormat(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

Supported raster image formats.

Members:

PNG: Portable Network Graphics (lossless). JPG: JPEG (lossy, smaller file size). SVG: Scalable Vector Graphics (vector).

Figure element — wraps a matplotlib figure, backend-agnostic.

class reporting.elements.figure.FigureElement(figure: object = None, **kwargs: object)[source]

Bases: BaseElement

A matplotlib figure embedded as a content element.

The figure is rendered to raster (PNG) or vector format (PDF for ReportLab, SVG for HTML) at render time.

Output format:

  • "png" (default) — raster image at dpi resolution (works with all renderers)

  • "pdf" — vector PDF via pdfrw (PDF renderer only; requires pip install pdfrw)

  • "svg" — vector SVG (HTML renderer only)

Container-relative sizing:

When container_width_pct or container_height_pct is set, the figure’s size is computed as a percentage of the container (panel) dimensions instead of the matplotlib figsize.

When preserve_aspect is True, the figure is scaled uniformly to fit within the content rect, anchored per cell alignment (default False = stretch to fill).

Parameters:

  • figure – A matplotlib.figure.Figure instance.

  • **kwargs –

    Property overrides:

    • dpi: int output resolution for raster formats (default 150)

    • bbox_inches: Optional[str] bbox setting for the save operation (default "tight")

    • format: str — "png", "pdf", or "svg" (default "png")

    • container_width_pct: Optional[float] width as a % of the container (default None)

    • container_height_pct: Optional[float] height as a % of the container (default None)

    • preserve_aspect: bool maintain aspect ratio when scaling (default False)

Example:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from reporting.elements.figure import FigureElement

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 3))
ax.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])

el = FigureElement(fig, format="pdf", preserve_aspect=True)
el2 = FigureElement(fig, container_width_pct=80)

Table element — wraps a pandas DataFrame, backend-agnostic.

class reporting.elements.table.ConditionalFormat(column: str = '', highlight_max: bool = False, highlight_min: bool = False, color_map: str | None = None, threshold: float | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A single conditional formatting rule for a table column.

Parameters:

  • column – Column name to apply the rule to.

  • highlight_max – Highlight the maximum value (default False).

  • highlight_min – Highlight the minimum value (default False).

  • color_map – Matplotlib colour map name for heatmap styling (e.g. "YlOrRd") (default None).

  • threshold – Numeric threshold for the rule (default None).

class reporting.elements.table.TableElement(data: object = None, **kwargs: object)[source]

Bases: BaseElement

A table created from a pandas DataFrame.

Parameters:

  • data – A pandas DataFrame.

  • **kwargs –

    Property overrides:

    • include_index: bool show the DataFrame index column (default False)

    • header: bool show a header row (default True)

    • zebra: bool alternate row background colours (default False)

    • numeric_format: Optional[str] format string for numeric values, e.g. "{:.2f}" (default None)

    • column_widths: Optional[list[float]] per-column widths in points (default None)

Example:

import pandas as pd
from reporting.elements.table import TableElement

df = pd.DataFrame({"A": [1, 2], "B": [3, 4]})
el = TableElement(df, zebra=True, numeric_format="{:.2f}")
el.highlight_max("A")
heatmap(column: str, color_map: str = 'YlOrRd') → TableElement[source]

Apply a heatmap colour gradient to a column.

Parameters:

  • column – Column name.

  • color_map – Matplotlib colour map name (default "YlOrRd").

Returns:

self for chaining.

highlight_max(column: str) → TableElement[source]

Highlight the maximum value in a column.

Parameters:

column – Column name.

Returns:

self for chaining.

highlight_min(column: str) → TableElement[source]

Highlight the minimum value in a column.

Parameters:

column – Column name.

Returns:

self for chaining.

Container element — allows nesting a Grid inside another Grid.

class reporting.elements.container.ContainerElement(grid: Grid | None = None, **kwargs: object)[source]

Bases: BaseElement

A container that holds a sub-grid within a single cell.

Used to place mixed content (text, image, plot, table) inside one grid cell by nesting another Grid inside it.

Parameters:

grid – A Grid instance defining the sub-layout (default None).

Example:

from reporting.layout.grid import Grid
from reporting.layout.sizing import Fill, Px
from reporting.elements.container import ContainerElement

inner = Grid(rows=2, cols=1,
             row_sizes=[Fill, Px(50)])
inner[0, 0].text("Top")
inner[1, 0].text("Bottom")

el = ContainerElement(inner)
set_grid(grid: Grid) → None[source]

Set or replace the sub-grid.

Parameters:

grid – A Grid instance.

Spacer element — occupies empty space in a layout.

class reporting.elements.spacer.SpacerElement(width: float = 0.0, height: float = 0.0, **kwargs: object)[source]

Bases: BaseElement

An invisible element that reserves space in a layout.

Useful for creating blank rows / columns or fine-tuning alignment without padding.

Parameters:

  • width – Horizontal space in points (default 0.0).

  • height – Vertical space in points (default 0.0).

Example:

from reporting.elements.spacer import SpacerElement

el = SpacerElement(width=20, height=10)

Styles

Color system — defines colors and palettes for themes.

class reporting.styles.colors.Color(hex: str, r: int, g: int, b: int, alpha: float = 1.0)[source]

Bases: object

Immutable colour stored as a hex string and integer RGB components.

Use Color.parse() to auto-detect any supported input format.

Parameters:

  • hex – Hex colour string (with or without #).

  • r – Red component (0..255).

  • g – Green component (0..255).

  • b – Blue component (0..255).

  • alpha – Opacity from 0.0 (transparent) to 1.0 (opaque) (default 1.0).

Example:

from reporting.styles.colors import Color

c = Color.parse("#1F4E79")
c2 = Color.from_rgb(31, 78, 121)
c3 = Color.parse("navy")
c4 = Color.parse((31, 78, 121))
property css: str

CSS-compatible hex string starting with #.

Example:

Color.parse("#1F4E79").css  # "#1F4E79"
property float_rgb: tuple[float, float, float]

RGB components as floats in [0, 1] (for ReportLab et al.).

static from_hex(hex_str: str, alpha: float = 1.0) → Color[source]

Create a Color from a hex string.

Accepts 3-digit ("#F00"), 6-digit ("#FF0000"), with or without leading #.

Parameters:

  • hex_str – Hex colour string.

  • alpha – Opacity (default 1.0).

Returns:

A Color instance.

static from_rgb(r: int, g: int, b: int, alpha: float = 1.0) → Color[source]

Create a Color from integer RGB components.

Parameters:

  • r – Red component (0..255).

  • g – Green component (0..255).

  • b – Blue component (0..255).

  • alpha – Opacity (default 1.0).

Returns:

A Color instance.

property hue: float

Hue angle in degrees (0..360) in the HSL model.

property lightness: float

Lightness in [0, 1] in the HSL model.

static parse(value: str | tuple[int, int, int] | Color, alpha: float = 1.0) → Color[source]

Auto-detect color format and return a Color.

Accepts:

  • Color instance — returned as-is (alpha updated if given)

  • hex string "#FF0000" or "FF0000"

  • named color "red", "light blue" (case/space insensitive)

  • "rgb(r, g, b)"

  • tuple[int, int, int] like (255, 0, 0)

property reportlab_color

Return a reportlab.lib.colors.Color object.

Requires reportlab to be installed.

property rgba: str

CSS rgba() string.

Example:

Color.parse("#1F4E79", alpha=0.5).rgba  # "rgba(31,78,121,0.5)"
property saturation: float

Saturation in [0, 1] in the HSL model.

static sort_by_hue(items: list[tuple[str, str]]) → list[tuple[str, str]][source]

Sort (name, hex) pairs by hue, then lightness, then saturation.

Parameters:

items – List of (colour_name, hex_value) tuples.

Returns:

A new list sorted by hue, lightness, saturation.

with_alpha(alpha: float) → Color[source]

Return a new Color with the given opacity.

Parameters:

alpha – Opacity from 0.0 to 1.0.

Returns:

A new Color with the updated alpha.

class reporting.styles.colors.ColorPalette(primary: Color, secondary: Color, accent: Color, background: Color, text_primary: Color, text_secondary: Color, border: Color, error: Color, warning: Color, success: Color)[source]

Bases: object

A set of named colours defining a visual theme.

Parameters:

  • primary – Brand primary colour.

  • secondary – Brand secondary colour.

  • accent – Accent / call-to-action colour.

  • background – Page / slide background colour.

  • text_primary – Primary text colour.

  • text_secondary – Secondary / muted text colour.

  • border – Border and divider colour.

  • error – Error / danger colour.

  • warning – Warning / caution colour.

  • success – Success / positive colour.

Example:

from reporting.styles.colors import ColorPalette, Color

palette = ColorPalette(
    primary=Color.from_hex("#1F4E79"),
    secondary=Color.from_hex("#2E75B6"),
    accent=Color.from_hex("#ED7D31"),
    background=Color.from_hex("#FFFFFF"),
    text_primary=Color.from_hex("#333333"),
    text_secondary=Color.from_hex("#666666"),
    border=Color.from_hex("#D9D9D9"),
    error=Color.from_hex("#C00000"),
    warning=Color.from_hex("#FFC000"),
    success=Color.from_hex("#70AD47"),
)
reporting.styles.colors.ColorValue

hex string "#RRGGBB", named CSS colour, (r, g, b) tuple, or Color instance.

Type:

Any value that can be resolved to a colour

alias of str | tuple[int, int, int] | Color

reporting.styles.colors.normalize_color(value: str | tuple[int, int, int] | Color) → str[source]

Normalize any colour input to a canonical hex string with #.

Parameters:

value – Any supported colour value.

Returns:

Hex string like "#1F4E79".

Typography system — defines font configurations for themes.

class reporting.styles.typography.FontSpec(family: str = 'Helvetica', size: float = 12.0, bold: bool = False, italic: bool = False, color: ColorValue | None = None, underline: bool = False)[source]

Bases: object

A single font specification (family, size, style, colour).

Used within a Typography to define the visual style for each text level (heading, body, caption, etc.).

Parameters:

  • family – Font family name (default "Helvetica").

  • size – Font size in points (default 12.0).

  • bold – Whether the font is bold (default False).

  • italic – Whether the font is italic (default False).

  • color – Text colour as a ColorValue (default None = inherit).

  • underline – Whether the text is underlined (default False).

Example:

from reporting.styles.typography import FontSpec

spec = FontSpec(family="Arial", size=11, color="#333333")
class reporting.styles.typography.Typography(heading_1: FontSpec = <factory>, heading_2: FontSpec = <factory>, heading_3: FontSpec = <factory>, body: FontSpec = <factory>, caption: FontSpec = <factory>, code: FontSpec = <factory>, mono: FontSpec = <factory>)[source]

Bases: object

A complete typography set for a report theme.

Defines font specifications for headings (three levels), body text, captions, code blocks, and monospace.

Parameters:

  • heading_1 – Top-level heading font (default: Helvetica 28pt bold).

  • heading_2 – Second-level heading font (default: Helvetica 22pt bold).

  • heading_3 – Third-level heading font (default: Helvetica 18pt bold).

  • body – Body text font (default: Helvetica 12pt).

  • caption – Caption / figure label font (default: Helvetica 10pt).

  • code – Inline code font (default: Courier 10pt).

  • mono – Code block / monospace font (default: Courier 11pt).

Example:

from reporting.styles.typography import Typography, FontSpec

typo = Typography(
    heading_1=FontSpec(family="Arial", size=28, bold=True,
                       color="#1F4E79"),
    body=FontSpec(family="Arial", size=11, color="#333333"),
)

Style primitives for TableSpec — CellStyle, ColumnStyle, RowStyle, TableStyle.

All style dataclasses are frozen (immutable) to prevent accidental sharing of mutable style references across cells, columns, or rows.

class reporting.tablespec.style.CellStyle(background_color: ColorValue | None = None, text_color: ColorValue | None = None, border_color: ColorValue | None = None, border_width: float | None = None, font_name: str | None = None, font_size: float | None = None, alignment: TextAlignment | None = None, padding: Edges | None = None, bold: bool | None = None, italic: bool | None = None, underline: bool | None = None, align_h: str | None = None, align_v: str | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

Per-cell styling overrides within a TableSpec.

Every field defaults to None, meaning “use parent/default” (inherits from RowStyle → ColumnStyle → TableStyle).

Parameters:

  • background_color – Cell background colour (default None).

  • text_color – Cell text colour (default None).

  • border_color – Cell border colour (default None).

  • border_width – Cell border width in points (default None).

  • font_name – Font family (default None).

  • font_size – Font size in points (default None).

  • alignment – TextAlignment (default None).

  • padding – Edges padding (default None).

  • bold – Bold override (default None).

  • italic – Italic override (default None).

  • underline – Underline override (default None).

  • align_h – Horizontal alignment string — "left", "center", "right" (default None).

  • align_v – Vertical alignment string — "top", "middle", "bottom" (default None).

class reporting.tablespec.style.ColumnStyle(background_color: ColorValue | None = None, text_color: ColorValue | None = None, border_color: ColorValue | None = None, border_width: float | None = None, font_name: str | None = None, font_size: float | None = None, alignment: TextAlignment | None = None, padding: Edges | None = None, bold: bool | None = None, italic: bool | None = None, underline: bool | None = None, align_h: str | None = None, align_v: str | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

Per-column styling defaults applied to each cell in the column.

Fields match CellStyle; all default to None.

Parameters:

  • background_color – Column background colour (default None).

  • text_color – Column text colour (default None).

  • border_color – Column border colour (default None).

  • border_width – Border width in points (default None).

  • font_name – Font family (default None).

  • font_size – Font size (default None).

  • alignment – TextAlignment (default None).

  • padding – Edges padding (default None).

  • bold – Bold override (default None).

  • italic – Italic override (default None).

  • underline – Underline override (default None).

  • align_h – Horizontal alignment string (default None).

  • align_v – Vertical alignment string (default None).

class reporting.tablespec.style.RowStyle(background_color: ColorValue | None = None, text_color: ColorValue | None = None, border_color: ColorValue | None = None, border_width: float | None = None, font_name: str | None = None, font_size: float | None = None, alignment: TextAlignment | None = None, padding: Edges | None = None, bold: bool | None = None, italic: bool | None = None, underline: bool | None = None, align_h: str | None = None, align_v: str | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

Per-row styling defaults applied to each cell in the row.

Fields match CellStyle; all default to None.

Parameters:

  • background_color – Row background colour (default None).

  • text_color – Row text colour (default None).

  • border_color – Row border colour (default None).

  • border_width – Border width in points (default None).

  • font_name – Font family (default None).

  • font_size – Font size (default None).

  • alignment – TextAlignment (default None).

  • padding – Edges padding (default None).

  • bold – Bold override (default None).

  • italic – Italic override (default None).

  • underline – Underline override (default None).

  • align_h – Horizontal alignment string (default None).

  • align_v – Vertical alignment string (default None).

class reporting.tablespec.style.TableStyle(zebra: bool = False, even_row_color: ColorValue = '#E8E8E8', odd_row_color: ColorValue = '#FFFFFF', header_background: ColorValue = '#4472C4', header_text_color: ColorValue = '#FFFFFF', border_color: ColorValue = '#D9D9D9', border_width: float = 0.5, font_name: str = 'Helvetica', font_size: float = 10.0, header_font_size: float = 11.0, padding: Edges = <factory>, highlight_max_color: ColorValue = '#C6EFCE', highlight_min_color: ColorValue = '#FFC7CE', header_rows: int = 1, min_font_size: float = 4.0, line_height: float = 1.2)[source]

Bases: object

Top-level table-wide style defaults for a TableSpec.

These values apply to the entire table unless overridden by a RowStyle, ColumnStyle, or CellStyle.

Parameters:

  • zebra – Enable alternating row colours (default False).

  • even_row_color – Even-row background colour (default "#E8E8E8").

  • odd_row_color – Odd-row background colour (default "#FFFFFF").

  • header_background – Header row background colour (default "#4472C4").

  • header_text_color – Header row text colour (default "#FFFFFF").

  • border_color – Grid border colour (default "#D9D9D9").

  • border_width – Grid border width in points (default 0.5).

  • font_name – Base font family (default "Helvetica").

  • font_size – Base font size in points (default 10.0).

  • header_font_size – Header font size in points (default 11.0).

  • padding – Cell padding as Edges (default Edges.all(4.0)).

  • highlight_max_color – Colour for highlight_max (default "#C6EFCE").

  • highlight_min_color – Colour for highlight_min (default "#FFC7CE").

  • header_rows – Number of header rows at the top (default 1).

  • min_font_size – Minimum font size for SHRINK_FONT mode (default 4.0).

  • line_height – Multiplier for row height estimation (default 1.2).

Example:

from reporting.tablespec.style import TableStyle

style = TableStyle(
    zebra=True,
    font_name="Arial",
    font_size=9.0,
    header_background="#1F4E79",
)
line_height: float = 1.2

Multiplier applied to font size to estimate row height.

min_font_size: float = 4.0

Minimum font size in points for SHRINK_FONT mode.

Theme system — defines visual themes for reports.

class reporting.styles.theme.CorporateTheme[source]

Bases: Theme

Default corporate theme (blue/grey palette, Arial fonts).

Uses the corporate colour scheme and Arial/Calibri fonts with a white background.

Example:

from reporting.styles.theme import CorporateTheme

theme = CorporateTheme()
class reporting.styles.theme.DarkTheme[source]

Bases: Theme

Dark theme (blue accent on dark background).

Uses Helvetica fonts with light text on a near-black background. Suited for presentations in dim environments.

Example:

from reporting.styles.theme import DarkTheme

theme = DarkTheme()
class reporting.styles.theme.LightTheme[source]

Bases: Theme

Light theme (blue on near-white background).

Similar to the corporate theme but with a slightly softer palette and a light grey background.

Example:

from reporting.styles.theme import LightTheme

theme = LightTheme()
class reporting.styles.theme.Theme(name: str, palette: ColorPalette, typography: Typography, table_style: TableStyle, footer: FooterConfig = <factory>, slide_types: dict[str, SlideTypeConfig]=<factory>)[source]

Bases: object

Base report theme combining colours, typography, and table styling.

Subclasses (CorporateTheme, DarkTheme, LightTheme) provide pre-built palettes. A custom theme can be created by instantiating Theme directly with any ColorPalette, Typography, and TableStyle.

Parameters:

  • name – Theme name for identification.

  • palette – The colour palette.

  • typography – Font specifications for all text levels.

  • table_style – Table style rules (column widths, zebra, etc.).

  • footer – Footer configuration (height, separator, font).

Example:

from reporting.styles.theme import Theme
from reporting.styles.colors import ColorPalette, Color
from reporting.styles.typography import Typography, FontSpec
from reporting.tablespec.style import TableStyle

theme = Theme(
    name="Custom",
    palette=ColorPalette(
        primary=Color.from_hex("#333"),
        secondary=Color.from_hex("#666"),
        # ... other colours
    ),
    typography=Typography(),
    table_style=TableStyle(),
)
get_heading_style(level: int) → FontSpec[source]

Get the font specification for a heading level.

Parameters:

level – 1, 2, or 3 for the corresponding heading.

Returns:

The FontSpec for that level, or body if the level is outside 1..3.

get_slide_type(name: str = 'default') → SlideTypeConfig[source]

Look up a slide type by name, falling back to a default if missing.

Parameters:

name – The slide type name.

Returns:

The matching SlideTypeConfig or a fresh default.

TableSpec

TableSpec — professional table data model.

Zero pandas/polars dependency; those are optional data sources loaded via classmethods with try/except ImportError.

class reporting.tablespec.spec.RangeSelector(spec: TableSpec, r1: int, c1: int, r2: int, c2: int)[source]

Bases: object

A selected cell range returned by TableSpec.range().

Supports chained .style(**kwargs) and .merge() calls.

Do not instantiate directly; obtain via table.range("A1:C4").

Example:

table.range("A1:D4").style(background_color="#D9EAF7", bold=True)
table.range("A1:D1").merge()
merge() → RangeSelector[source]

Merge the range: set colspan/rowspan on the top-left cell.

Returns:

self for chaining.

style(**kwargs: Any) → RangeSelector[source]

Apply a CellStyle built from kwargs to every cell in the range.

Parameters:

**kwargs – CellStyle field values (background_color, bold, etc.).

Returns:

self for chaining.

class reporting.tablespec.spec.TableSpec(columns: list[Column] | None = None, rows: list[TableRow] | None = None, style: TableStyle | None = None, sizing: TableSizing | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A flexible, backend-agnostic table data model.

TableSpec supports merged cells, per-cell styling, deferred conditional formatting, Excel-style cell access, and multiple data-source constructors (DataFrame, Polars, dataclasses, records).

Sizing modes: STRETCH (default), SHRINK_FONT (shrink font to fit), PERCENT (occupy a percentage of the available width).

Parameters:

  • columns – List of Column definitions.

  • rows – List of TableRow instances (default None).

  • style – TableStyle instance (default TableStyle()).

  • sizing – TableSizing instance (default TableSizing()).

Example:

from reporting.tablespec import TableSpec, Column

table = TableSpec(
    columns=[
        Column("Case"),
        Column("Mach"),
        Column("Efficiency"),
    ]
)
table.add_row("Case A", 0.80, 0.92)
table.add_row("Case B", 0.90, 0.94)

# Excel-style access
table["A1"] = "Results"
table.merge("A1:D1")
add_column(name: str, label: str | None = None, width: float | None = None, width_ratio: float | None = None, format: str | None = None, formatter: Callable[[Any], str] | None = None, alignment: object | None = None, visible: bool = True, style: object | None = None) → TableSpec[source]

Append a column definition and return self for chaining.

Parameters:

  • name – Column identifier (must be unique).

  • label – Header display text (defaults to name).

  • width – Fixed width in points (default None).

  • width_ratio – Relative width factor (default None).

  • format – Python format string e.g. "{:.2f}" (default None).

  • formatter – Custom callable (value) -> str (default None).

  • alignment – Default text alignment (default None).

  • visible – Whether the column appears in output (default True).

  • style – ColumnStyle or None (default None).

Returns:

self for chaining.

add_condition(column: str | int, rule: Callable[[Any], bool], **style_kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Add a conditional format rule for column.

The rule callable receives the cell value; when it returns True, the style_kwargs are applied as a CellStyle.

Examples:

table.add_condition("Temperature", lambda v: v > 1200,
                    background_color="red")
table.add_condition("Efficiency", lambda v: v > 0.90,
                    background_color="green")
add_heatmap(column: str | int, min_color: str = '#FFFFCC', max_color: str = '#FF0000') → TableSpec[source]

Add a heatmap for column (deferred — resolved at render time).

add_highlight_max(column: str | int, color: str = '#C6EFCE') → TableSpec[source]

Add a highlight-max rule for column (deferred — resolved at render time).

add_highlight_min(column: str | int, color: str = '#FFC7CE') → TableSpec[source]

Add a highlight-min rule for column (deferred — resolved at render time).

add_row(*values: Any, **kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Add a row and return self for chaining.

Accepts positional values (one per column) or keyword arguments mapping column names to values (not both).

Parameters:

  • *values – One value per column, in order.

  • **kwargs – Column-name → value mappings.

Returns:

self for chaining.

Raises:

InvalidRowError – if both positional and keyword arguments are provided.

apply_style(range_ref: str, **kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Apply a CellStyle built from kwargs to every cell in the range.

Examples:

table.apply_style("A1:D1", background_color="#003366",
                  text_color="white", bold=True)
table.apply_style("B2:C5", background_color="#D9EAF7")
classmethod build() → TableBuilder[source]

Return a TableBuilder for chained construction.

Example:

table = TableSpec.build().columns("a","b").add_row(1,2).build()
cell(row: int, col: int, value: Any = None, text: str | None = None, rowspan: int = 1, colspan: int = 1, alignment: object | None = None, background_color: str | None = None, text_color: str | None = None, style: object | None = None) → TableSpec[source]

Modify a specific cell in the table.

Useful for setting spans or per-cell overrides after row insertion.

column(name: str) → Column[source]

Return the Column with the given name (for chained configuration).

Usage:

table.column("Mach").set_format("{:.3f}")
table.column("Efficiency").set_formatter(lambda x: f"{x*100:.1f}%")
classmethod from_dataclasses(instances: list[Any], **kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Build a TableSpec from a list of dataclass instances.

Field names become column names.

classmethod from_dataframe(df: Any, **kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Build a TableSpec from a pandas DataFrame.

Parameters:

  • df – A pandas.DataFrame.

  • **kwargs – Passed through to TableSpec().

Raises:

ImportError – if pandas is not installed.

classmethod from_polars(df: Any, **kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Build a TableSpec from a Polars DataFrame.

Parameters:

  • df – A polars.DataFrame or polars.LazyFrame.

  • **kwargs – Passed through to TableSpec().

Raises:

ImportError – if polars is not installed.

classmethod from_records(records: list[dict[str, Any]], **kwargs: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Build a TableSpec from a list of dicts (records).

Column order is inferred from the first record’s keys.

group(name: str) → TableSpec[source]

Set the current group name.

All subsequent add_row() calls will belong to this group until group() is called again with a different name.

heatmap(column: str) → TableSpec[source]

Apply a green-to-red heatmap to column.

highlight_max(column: str) → TableSpec[source]

Highlight the maximum value in column with a green background.

highlight_min(column: str) → TableSpec[source]

Highlight the minimum value in column with a red background.

merge(range_ref: str) → TableSpec[source]

Merge cells in an Excel-style range.

Sets colspan/rowspan on the top-left cell of the range.

Examples:

table.merge("A1:D1")     # merge header across 4 columns
table.merge("A1:A4")     # merge label across 4 rows
range(range_ref: str) → RangeSelector[source]

Return a RangeSelector for chained operations on a range.

Examples:

table.range("A1:D4").style(background_color="#D9EAF7", bold=True)
table.range("A1:D1").merge()
resolve_cell_style(row_idx: int, col_idx: int, cell_idx: int | None = None) → CellStyle[source]

Resolve the effective CellStyle for a cell by merging all layers.

Merge order (last wins): TableStyle → RowStyle → ColumnStyle → CellStyle. Then applies deferred conditionals/heatmaps/highlights. Finally applies inline background_color/text_color overrides.

Parameters:

  • row_idx – Row index.

  • col_idx – Visual column index (for column lookups).

  • cell_idx – Cell index in the row (defaults to col_idx). Use this when rowspan/colspan causes visual and cell indices to differ.

row(*items: Any) → TableSpec[source]

Fluent row builder. Accepts a mix of TableCell and plain values.

Examples:

table.row("Case A", 0.80, 0.92)
table.row(cell("Header", colspan=3))
to_dataframe() → Any[source]

Export to a pandas DataFrame.

Raises:

ImportError – if pandas is not installed.

to_dict() → dict[str, Any][source]

Serialize to a JSON-compatible dict.

Returns:

Dictionary with keys "columns", "rows", and "style".

to_json(indent: int = 2) → str[source]

Serialize to a JSON string.

to_records() → list[dict[str, Any]][source]

Export rows as a list of dicts (column name -> display text).

zebra() → TableSpec[source]

Enable alternating row colors.

Column entity — defines a single column’s schema and style.

class reporting.tablespec.column.Column(name: str, label: str | None = None, width: float | None = None, width_ratio: float | None = None, format: str | None = None, formatter: Callable[[Any], str] | None = None, alignment: TextAlignment | None = None, visible: bool = True, style: ColumnStyle | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A column definition within a TableSpec.

Parameters:

  • name – Column identifier (must be unique within a table).

  • label – Visual header text; defaults to name if not provided (default None).

  • width – Fixed width in points (default None).

  • width_ratio – Relative width factor (e.g. 2.0 = twice the default; default None).

  • format – Python format string for display, e.g. "{:.2%}" (default None).

  • formatter – Custom callable (value) -> display_text (default None).

  • alignment – Default TextAlignment for cells (default None).

  • visible – Whether the column appears in rendered output (default True).

  • style – Per-column ColumnStyle overrides (default None).

Example:

from reporting.tablespec.column import Column

col = Column("Efficiency",
             label="Eff. (%)",
             format="{:.1%}",
             width=80)
col2 = Column("Temperature",
              formatter=lambda v: f"{v:.0f}°C")
set_format(fmt: str) → Column[source]

Set the format string and return self for chaining.

Parameters:

fmt – Python format string (e.g. "{:.3f}").

Returns:

self for chaining.

set_formatter(func: Callable[[Any], str]) → Column[source]

Set a custom formatter callable and return self for chaining.

Parameters:

func – A callable (value) -> display_text.

Returns:

self for chaining.

TableRow — a single row within a TableSpec.

reporting.tablespec.row.Row

alias of TableRow

class reporting.tablespec.row.TableRow(cells: list[TableCell] = <factory>, style: RowStyle | None = None, group: str | None = None, metadata: dict[str, ~typing.Any]=<factory>)[source]

Bases: object

A single row in a TableSpec table.

Rows are created automatically by TableSpec.add_row() and TableSpec.row().

Parameters:

  • cells – List of TableCell objects, one per visible column (default empty list).

  • style – Per-row RowStyle overrides (default None).

  • group – Group name for hierarchical / grouped tables (default None).

  • metadata – Arbitrary key-value metadata attached to this row (default empty dict).

Example:

from reporting.tablespec.row import TableRow
from reporting.tablespec.cell import TableCell

row = TableRow(cells=[
    TableCell(value="Case A"),
    TableCell(value=0.85),
])

TableCell — a single cell within a TableSpec.

reporting.tablespec.cell.Cell

alias of TableCell

class reporting.tablespec.cell.TableCell(value: Any = None, text: str | None = None, rowspan: int = 1, colspan: int = 1, alignment: TextAlignment | None = None, background_color: ColorValue | None = None, text_color: ColorValue | None = None, style: CellStyle | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

A single cell in a TableSpec table.

Cells are created automatically by TableSpec.add_row(). For merged cells, set rowspan / colspan after creation.

Parameters:

  • value – The raw cell value (any type; default None).

  • text – Display text. If None, the TableSpec computes it from value and the column’s format / formatter (default None).

  • rowspan – Number of rows this cell spans (>= 1; default 1).

  • colspan – Number of columns this cell spans (>= 1; default 1).

  • alignment – Per-cell alignment override (default None).

  • background_color – Per-cell background colour override (default None).

  • text_color – Per-cell text colour override (default None).

  • style – Per-cell CellStyle override (default None).

Example:

from reporting.tablespec.cell import TableCell

cell = TableCell(value=42, text="42.0")
merged = TableCell(value="Header", colspan=3)
property display_text: str

Return the display text or stringified value.

Returns:

The explicit text if set, otherwise str(value), otherwise "".

Table sizing modes — TableFitMode, ColumnDistrib, and TableSizing.

Controls how a table occupies its allocated space:

  • STRETCH: fill available space, keep font size unchanged.

  • SHRINK_FONT: reduce font size (down to min_font_size) if content overflows the available height or width.

  • PERCENT: occupy percent_width of the available width; the container (Panel with HAlign) handles alignment.

class reporting.tablespec.sizing.ColumnDistrib(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

How columns are distributed within the table’s width.

Members:

CONTENT

Proportional to measured text width (default).

EQUAL

All columns get equal width.

FIXED

Use Column.width where set; remaining space split equally.

class reporting.tablespec.sizing.TableFitMode(*values)[source]

Bases: Enum

How a table sizes itself within its allocated rectangle.

Members:
STRETCH: Fill available space. Column widths are

content-aware (proportional to text width). Font size stays at TableStyle.font_size. This is the default.

SHRINK_FONT: Start at TableStyle.font_size but

shrink the font proportionally if the natural content width or height exceeds the available space. Never goes below min_font_size.

PERCENT: Use percent_width of the available

width for column distribution. The container (Panel) handles alignment.

class reporting.tablespec.sizing.TableSizing(fit_mode: TableFitMode = TableFitMode.STRETCH, min_font_size: float | None = None, percent_width: float = 1.0, column_distrib: ColumnDistrib = ColumnDistrib.CONTENT)[source]

Bases: object

Per-table sizing configuration for a TableSpec.

min_font_size here overrides TableStyle.min_font_size. percent_width is only meaningful when fit_mode == PERCENT.

Parameters:

  • fit_mode – The sizing strategy (default TableFitMode.STRETCH).

  • min_font_size – Override TableStyle.min_font_size for this table (default None).

  • percent_width – Fraction of available width to use when fit_mode == PERCENT (default 1.0).

  • column_distrib – How columns are distributed within the table width (default ColumnDistrib.CONTENT).

Example:

from reporting.tablespec.sizing import (
    TableSizing, TableFitMode, ColumnDistrib,
)

sizing = TableSizing(
    fit_mode=TableFitMode.SHRINK_FONT,
    min_font_size=5.0,
    column_distrib=ColumnDistrib.FIXED,
)

Renderers

Abstract base renderer — defines the renderer interface.

class reporting.renderers.base.BaseRenderer[source]

Bases: ABC

Abstract base class for all output renderers.

Subclasses must implement render_document, render_slide, and render_panel. A renderer converts the report model (Document → Slide → Panel → BaseElement) into an output format (PDF, HTML, etc.).

Example:

from reporting.renderers.base import BaseRenderer

class MyRenderer(BaseRenderer):
    def render_document(self, document, output_path):
        ...
    def render_slide(self, slide):
        ...
    def render_panel(self, panel, rect):
        ...
abstractmethod render_document(document: Document, output_path: str) → None[source]

Render an entire Document to an output file.

Parameters:

  • document – The report document to render.

  • output_path – File path for the output (e.g. "report.pdf").

abstractmethod render_panel(panel: object, rect: object) → object[source]

Render a single panel (cell) within a slide.

Parameters:

  • panel – A Panel instance.

  • rect – The Rect bounding the panel in pixels.

Returns:

The panel object (or None).

abstractmethod render_slide(slide: object) → object[source]

Render a single Slide.

Parameters:

slide – A Slide instance.

Returns:

The slide object (or a renderer-specific handle).

PDF renderer — generates PDF output using ReportLab with absolute positioning.

class reporting.renderers.pdf.renderer.PDFRenderer[source]

Bases: BaseRenderer

Render a report to PDF using ReportLab with absolute positioning.

Each Slide becomes a PDF page. The title panel is rendered at the top, followed by the grid layout with all elements positioned absolutely.

Supported element types: TextElement, ImageElement, FigureElement, TableElement, TableSpecElement, ContainerElement.

Example:

from reporting.document import Document
from reporting.renderers.pdf.renderer import PDFRenderer

doc = Document()
slide = doc.new_slide("Results")
slide.grid_layout(rows=1, cols=1)
slide[0, 0].text("Hello, PDF!")

renderer = PDFRenderer()
renderer.render_document(doc, "output.pdf")
render_document(document: Document, output_path: str) → None[source]

Render all slides of a document to a single PDF file.

Each slide becomes one page. Temporary figure images are cleaned up after rendering.

Parameters:

  • document – The report document.

  • output_path – Output PDF file path (e.g. "report.pdf").

render_panel(panel: object, rect: object) → object[source]

Render a single panel (no-op in PDF renderer).

Panels are rendered as part of slide rendering via _render_element. This method exists for interface conformance.

Parameters:

  • panel – A Panel instance.

  • rect – A Rect bounding box.

Returns:

None.

render_slide(slide: object) → object[source]

Render a single slide (add to current PDF page, then showPage).

Parameters:

slide – A Slide instance.

Returns:

The slide object.

HTML renderer — generates HTML/CSS output.

class reporting.renderers.html.renderer.HTMLRenderer(standalone: bool = True)[source]

Bases: BaseRenderer

Render a report to a standalone HTML file with CSS styling.

Each Slide becomes a <div class="slide"> with absolute- positioned cells. Images and figure PNGs are embedded as base64 data URIs for portability.

Parameters:

standalone – If True (default), generates a complete HTML document with <!DOCTYPE html>, <head>, and inline CSS. If False, returns only the slide <div> fragments for embedding.

Example:

from reporting.document import Document
from reporting.renderers.html.renderer import HTMLRenderer

doc = Document()
slide = doc.new_slide("Hello")
slide.grid_layout(1, 1)
slide[0, 0].text("Hello, HTML!")

renderer = HTMLRenderer()
renderer.render_document(doc, "output.html")
render_document(document: Document, output_path: str) → None[source]

Render an entire Document to an output file.

Parameters:

  • document – The report document to render.

  • output_path – File path for the output (e.g. "report.pdf").

render_panel(panel: object, rect: object) → object[source]

Render a panel (no-op in HTML renderer).

Parameters:

  • panel – A Panel instance.

  • rect – A Rect bounding box.

Returns:

None.

render_slide(slide: object) → object[source]

Render a single slide as HTML.

Parameters:

slide – A Slide instance.

Returns:

The HTML string for the slide.

Utils

Helper utilities for matplotlib figure handling.

reporting.utils.figure_helpers.figure_to_bytes(fig: object, dpi: int = 150, fmt: str = 'png') → bytes[source]

Render a matplotlib figure to bytes.

Parameters:

  • fig – A matplotlib.figure.Figure.

  • dpi – Output resolution (default 150).

  • fmt – Output format — "png", "pdf", "svg" (default "png").

Returns:

The rendered image as bytes.

reporting.utils.figure_helpers.figure_to_pdf_bytes(fig: object) → bytes[source]

Render a matplotlib figure to PDF bytes.

Parameters:

fig – A matplotlib.figure.Figure.

Returns:

The PDF as bytes.

reporting.utils.figure_helpers.figure_to_svg_bytes(fig: object) → bytes[source]

Render a matplotlib figure to SVG bytes.

Parameters:

fig – A matplotlib.figure.Figure.

Returns:

The SVG as bytes.

reporting.utils.figure_helpers.figure_to_tempfile(fig: object, dpi: int = 150, suffix: str = '.png') → str[source]

Render a matplotlib figure to a temporary file.

The caller is responsible for deleting the file.

Parameters:

  • fig – A matplotlib.figure.Figure.

  • dpi – Output resolution (default 150).

  • suffix – File suffix (default ".png").

Returns:

Path to the temporary file.
